critic_raw:
The methodology is well-structured and shows awareness of many standard pitfalls. However, I identify several genuine methodological problems, organized by their root causes.

---

**Group A: Temporal Leakage via Random Stratified Split**

The single most serious flaw is the data partitioning strategy. The authors explicitly choose stratified random sampling at the message level rather than a temporal split, justifying this by wanting to avoid 'campaign bursts or quarter-specific reporting artifacts.' This reasoning inverts the actual risk.

**Claim:** Stratified random splitting at the message level keeps the label and domain distributions comparable across splits, producing a fair evaluation.

**Why it's wrong:** Email phishing is a temporally ordered process. Sender reputation, graph connectivity, and even text templates from a given campaign will appear in training, validation, and test sets simultaneously when the split is random. The graph features — sender in-degree, sender out-degree, sender reputation score, first-time sender flag — are computed from the full development corpus and then frozen. But if a sender appears in both training and test (because splitting is random across time), the model sees graph features derived from test-period activity during training, and the 'first-time sender' flag is computed on the full 18-month graph rather than on only the data that would have been available at inference time. This is not a minor concern: a sender who launched a campaign in month 14 will have their month-14 emails scattered across train/val/test, and the graph features for those test-set emails will reflect sender history that includes training-set activity from the same campaign. The model is then rewarded for learning a pattern that is only visible because future data leaked into graph construction.

The stated mitigation — 'coarse month-of-arrival composition within each split' — does not fix this. Preserving month composition within splits does not prevent individual campaign emails from being split across partitions. It only ensures the histogram of months is balanced, not that the information boundary is respected.

The justification (avoiding campaign bursts dominating one holdout) is legitimate, but the solution is not random splitting — it is a time-blocked split with some smoothing (e.g., leave-last-N-months-out) or at minimum strict temporal ordering of the test window.

**Evidence either way:** Rerun the experiment with a strict forward-chaining temporal split (train on months 1–12, validate on 13–15, test on 16–18) and compare to the stratified split results. If AUC drops meaningfully — especially on the 'new sender' subgroup — leakage is the explanation.

---

**Group B: Graph Feature Construction Creates Lookahead on Test Set**

This is related to but distinct from Group A.

**Claim:** Freezing graph statistics after a single construction pass prevents moving-target effects and keeps the structural representation consistent.

**Why it's wrong:** 'Frozen after construction' is not the same as 'computed from training data only.' The graph is built from 'the development corpus,' which includes all 18 months of messages before the random split is applied. This means graph features for test-set emails — sender in-degree, out-degree, reputation, and first-time-sender flag — are computed using information from test-set emails themselves (emails that arrived after the ones being scored in production would have, had this been a real deployment). The sender reputation feature is particularly damaging: it is defined as 'fraction of prior sender emails flagged as malicious.' If computed on the full corpus, a sender whose phishing is only flagged late in the campaign will have elevated reputation scores on their early test-set emails, scores that would not have been available at inference time in production.

The 'first-time sender flag' suffers similarly: a sender who appears 15 months in is marked as 'not first-time' on all their emails if the graph is built on the full corpus, even though at month 1 of their campaign they were genuinely novel.

**Evidence either way:** Reconstruct graph features using only information available before each email's timestamp (a point-in-time join), then compare performance. A meaningful drop in the graph model's advantage — particularly on sender reputation and first-time-sender features — would confirm lookahead contamination.

---

**Group C: Asymmetric Hyperparameter Search Inflates Joint Model's Apparent Advantage**

**Claim:** The joint model receives the full hyperparameter search; the text-only baseline uses a fixed configuration. This is stated as intentional — to keep the baseline 'focused on the value of text alone rather than on optimization breadth.'

**Why it's wrong:** This design directly conflates two things: (a) the benefit of graph features and (b) the benefit of hyperparameter optimization. If the joint model wins after a full search but the baseline was never tuned, the measured delta is an upper bound on graph feature value and a lower bound on baseline performance. The authors acknowledge this but frame it as a feature rather than a confound. It is not: it means the experiment cannot isolate whether the improvement comes from graph features, from a better learning rate, from a better dropout rate, or from some interaction. The stated goal — 'test whether joint encoding outperforms text-only' — requires both models to be given equal optimization opportunity.

A valid design would apply the same hyperparameter search to both models, or alternatively fix both to the same configuration. The current design only supports the weaker claim: 'a tuned joint model outperforms an untuned text-only model,' which is trivially expected.

**Evidence either way:** Run the text-only baseline through the same hyperparameter search. If the gap between joint and text-only shrinks substantially, the original comparison was measuring optimization advantage, not feature advantage.

---

**Group D: Label Construction Introduces Survivorship Bias with Temporal Interaction**

**Claim:** Requiring ≥2 independent confirmations for a positive label reduces noise and provides a reliable training signal.

**Why it's wrong:** Analyst closure and user-reported submissions are time-delayed processes. Emails from early in the 18-month window have had more time to accumulate confirmations than emails from the final months. Under a random split, recent emails — which are less likely to have accumulated ≥2 confirmations regardless of whether they are truly malicious — will appear in training, validation, and test sets alike but with systematically lower positive labeling rates. Under a temporal split this would be visible as a degraded test set label quality; under a random split it is masked, making model performance appear more stable than it is. More importantly, this creates a latent correlation between recency and 'labeled negative,' which the model may learn to exploit (recent = safer score), particularly through the first-time-sender flag which correlates with recency.

The authors note this as a known sensitivity reduction but do not analyze whether confirmation lag is uniform across the 18 months. If it is not — and it almost certainly is not — the conservative label rule has a temporal component that interacts badly with a random split.

**Evidence either way:** Plot the positive label rate by month of arrival. If it declines in the final 3–4 months (not due to actual campaign reduction but due to confirmation lag), this confirms the bias. Then examine whether the model's predicted scores for recent emails are systematically lower than for older emails with identical features.

---

**Group E: Test Set Used for Checkpoint Selection Violates Holdout Integrity**

**Claim:** After validation-stage selection, the held-out test partition is used for a 'final stability check' to break ties between checkpoints with similar validation performance.

**Why it's wrong:** The moment test-set information — even indirect information like 'lower variation in false-positive burden on the held-out partition' — influences checkpoint selection, the test set is no longer held out. The test set AUC reported at the end of the experiment is now a biased estimate of generalization performance because the test set participated in model selection. This is a textbook violation of holdout discipline. The authors frame it as a 'stability check' but the mechanism is identical to using the test set for selection: a checkpoint is chosen partly on the basis of its behavior on test data. Even if this tie-breaking is rare, the selection procedure must be defined before seeing any test data, not conditioned on it.

**Evidence either way:** This is a design flaw, not an empirical question. The fix is to remove the test-set stability check entirely and break ties using validation metrics only, or to use a separate held-out period for stability analysis.

---

**Summary of Verdict:**

Issues A and B (temporal leakage and graph feature lookahead) are the most severe and stem from the same root cause: the random stratified split prevents enforcing an information boundary between what the model could have known at inference time and what it uses during training and feature construction. Issue C (asymmetric search) undermines the core comparison the experiment is designed to make. Issue E (test set checkpoint selection) is a clear holdout violation. Together these are enough to conclude the methodology as designed cannot support its stated objective.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis of Each Critique Point

### Critique A: Stratified Random Splitting Causes Temporal Leakage via Campaign Co-occurrence

**Critic's claim:** Because stratified random splitting scatters campaign emails across train/val/test sets, graph features computed on the full 18-month corpus embed information from test-period activity into training-stage representations. The 'coarse month-of-arrival composition' preservation does not fix the information boundary problem.

**Assessment:** This critique is substantially valid and I must concede the core of it. The defense I would normally mount — that month-composition preservation approximates temporal ordering — fails upon careful inspection. Preserving the histogram of months within each split is not equivalent to preserving the information boundary. A sender who runs a campaign across months 13–16 will have individual emails scattered across all three splits. The graph features for those test-set emails reflect connectivity derived from training-set emails in the same campaign. This is not a theoretical concern: sender reputation is explicitly defined as 'fraction of prior sender emails flagged as malicious,' and computing that fraction on the full corpus before splitting means test-set emails carry reputation scores that incorporate confirmation outcomes from emails assigned to training. The practical consequence is that the graph features encode a form of label leakage — the reputation signal for a test email is elevated by the confirmed-malicious labels on training emails from the same campaign.

The stated justification — avoiding campaign burst domination of one holdout window — is a real concern, but the critic is correct that random splitting is the wrong solution. A time-blocked approach (train on months 1–12, validate on 13–15, test on 16–18) with some smoothing for campaign seasonality would address the burst concern without breaching the information boundary. Alternatively, a forward-chaining temporal split with explicit blocking of sender campaigns across the boundary would be more defensible.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** The magnitude of the leakage effect depends on how many campaigns span multiple split windows and how quickly analyst confirmations accumulate. In some enterprise environments, campaigns are short-lived and most confirmations arrive within two weeks, meaning the leakage could be modest. But the design cannot assert this without demonstrating it empirically — the design as written does not bound the risk.

**Concession:** Substantial. The critic has correctly identified that the justification for random splitting does not neutralize the temporal leakage risk. This is a genuine flaw.

---

### Critique B: Graph Feature Construction Uses Full Corpus, Creating Lookahead Contamination

**Critic's claim:** Graph features are frozen after construction on the 'development corpus,' but that corpus includes all 18 months of messages — meaning test-set emails' graph features are computed using information (including test-period sender behavior and confirmation outcomes) that would not have been available at inference time.

**Assessment:** This critique is correct and is, as the critic notes, distinct from Critique A even though they share a root cause. The design description says graph statistics are 'frozen once the feature-generation pass is complete' and cites this as preventing moving-target effects. But 'frozen' only prevents graph updates during model fitting; it does not prevent the initial construction from using lookahead data. The two problematic features are:

1. **Sender reputation** ('fraction of prior sender emails flagged as malicious'): If computed on the full corpus, an email sent in month 2 by a sender whose campaign is only confirmed in months 14–16 carries an elevated reputation score reflecting confirmations that did not exist at month 2. This is a direct form of label leakage through a feature, not just train/test contamination.

2. **First-time sender flag**: If the graph is built on the full 18-month corpus, a sender who first appears in month 15 is correctly marked as first-time — but a sender who first appears in month 2 and runs a long campaign would have that flag set to 'not first-time' on all their messages, including ones that, at training time, were genuinely from a novel sender. More importantly, under the random split, test-set emails from early in a campaign have the 'not first-time' flag reflecting later-campaign activity.

The correct design would require point-in-time feature construction: for each email, compute graph features using only emails with timestamps strictly earlier than the email being scored. This is computationally heavier but is the only way to avoid lookahead.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** The sender in-degree and out-degree features are less directly contaminated by label leakage (they reflect connectivity, not confirmed maliciousness), but they still embed future structural information. Whether this materially distorts the evaluation depends on how dynamic sender networks are over the 18-month window.

**Concession:** Full. The 'frozen after construction' framing in the design description obscures a real lookahead problem. The critic is correct.

---

### Critique C: Asymmetric Hyperparameter Search Conflates Feature Value with Optimization Advantage

**Critic's claim:** The joint model receives a full hyperparameter search; the text-only baseline uses a fixed configuration. The measured performance gap therefore reflects both the benefit of graph features and the benefit of additional optimization, making it impossible to isolate feature contribution.

**Assessment:** This critique is correct and the design's stated rationale is not a defense. The design says the asymmetric treatment is intentional — to keep the baseline 'focused on the value of text alone rather than on optimization breadth.' This framing is backwards. If the goal is to measure the value of text alone (as a feature set), the baseline needs to be given its best possible representation, which requires the same optimization effort. Restricting baseline optimization does not isolate text feature value; it understates baseline performance. The experiment's primary scientific question is 'does adding graph features improve performance beyond text alone?' This question can only be answered if both models are at approximately their optimum. A tuned joint model vs. an untuned text-only model answers a different question: 'is a well-tuned joint model better than a carelessly configured text-only model?' — which is not scientifically interesting.

The design explicitly acknowledges the asymmetry but frames it as a feature. I cannot defend this framing. The acknowledgment without correction is itself a problem: a known confound that is documented but not fixed is still a confound.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** It is possible that, even with equal optimization effort, the joint model wins by a similar margin. But we cannot claim this without running the symmetric comparison.

**Concession:** Full. This is a design error, not a design choice. The fix is to apply equivalent hyperparameter search to both models, or to fix both to the same configuration and avoid search entirely.

---

### Critique D: Confirmation Lag Creates Temporal Bias in Labels That Is Masked by Random Split

**Critic's claim:** Analyst closure and user-report accumulation are time-delayed. Emails from recent months are less likely to have accumulated ≥2 confirmations regardless of true maliciousness. Under a random split this bias is distributed across all sets and masked; under a temporal split it would be visible as degraded test-set label quality. The model may learn that recency correlates with benign scoring.

**Assessment:** This critique is valid in its core claim. Confirmation lag is a real phenomenon in security operations: emails reported in the final two to three months of an 18-month corpus are less likely to have received analyst closure simply because there has been less time. The conservative labeling rule (≥2 confirmations) amplifies this — recent true positives are more likely to be labeled negative. The design does acknowledge that the conservative rule reduces sensitivity, but it does not analyze whether confirmation lag is uniform across the 18 months. The critic is correct that it almost certainly is not uniform, and that this non-uniformity has a systematic direction (recent = fewer confirmations = lower positive rate in labels, even holding true prevalence constant).

However, I would moderate the severity assessment slightly relative to Critiques A and B. The confirmation lag problem is partially addressed by the design's acknowledgment that 'some true positives may be unlabeled.' The design is not claiming to measure absolute recall; it is claiming to measure comparative ranking quality (ROC-AUC) between two models trained on the same label set. If both models are trained on the same biased labels, and both are evaluated on the same biased test labels, the comparative AUC may still be meaningful for the relative question ('does graph context help?') even if the absolute AUC is inflated. The more serious interaction the critic identifies — that the model may learn recency as a proxy for benign — is real but requires empirical confirmation. The recommended diagnostic (plot positive label rate by month) is a valid and necessary check that the design does not include.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** Whether the confirmation lag bias is large enough to systematically shift model scores toward lower values for recent emails depends on the organization's analyst throughput and the typical time-to-closure for phishing cases. This is an empirical question.

**Concession:** Partial. The core claim is valid — confirmation lag creates a temporal label bias that is masked by random splitting and not analyzed in the design. The severity is somewhat lower than Critiques A/B/C because the comparative question may survive the bias if it affects both models equally, but the design should include the diagnostic.

---

### Critique E: Test Set Used for Checkpoint Selection Violates Holdout Integrity

**Critic's claim:** The design uses the held-out test partition to break ties between checkpoints with similar validation performance ('lower variation in false-positive burden on the held-out partition'). This means test-set information influences model selection, biasing the reported AUC.

**Assessment:** The critic is entirely correct, and this is a textbook holdout violation. The design describes the procedure as a 'final stability check' before 'locking the model for reporting,' but the mechanism is identical to using the test set for selection: a checkpoint is chosen — at least in the tie-breaking case — partly on the basis of its behavior on test data. The fact that this only applies when checkpoints are 'effectively tied' on validation does not rescue it. Even infrequent conditioning on test-set behavior corrupts the holdout's statistical independence. The test AUC reported after this procedure is not an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.

The fix is straightforward and the critic names it: remove the test-set stability check entirely. Tie-breaking should use validation metrics only (e.g., validation AUC averaged across multiple random seeds, or validation false-positive rate at a fixed recall). If stability analysis is scientifically important, it should be conducted on a separate held-aside period, not on the primary evaluation holdout.

**What remains genuinely uncertain:** Nothing. This is a design error with a clear fix. There is no empirical question here.

**Concession:** Full and immediate.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Summarizing the concessions from Pass 1:

- **Critique A (temporal leakage via random split):** Substantial concession. The random split does not enforce an information boundary; the month-composition preservation does not fix the problem.
- **Critique B (graph feature lookahead):** Full concession. 'Frozen after construction' does not prevent lookahead if construction uses the full corpus. Sender reputation and first-time sender flag are particularly contaminated.
- **Critique C (asymmetric hyperparameter search):** Full concession. The stated rationale is backwards; both models need equal optimization opportunity to answer the scientific question.
- **Critique D (confirmation lag and label bias):** Partial concession. The bias is real and unanalyzed, though its severity for the comparative question is uncertain.
- **Critique E (test set checkpoint selection):** Full concession. This is a textbook holdout violation with no defensible justification.

Four of five critique points receive full or substantial concessions. Critiques A, B, C, and E each independently constitute serious methodological problems that undermine the experiment's ability to answer its stated question. In combination, they mean the reported AUC differential between the joint and text-only models cannot be attributed to graph feature utility: it could reflect leakage (A, B), optimization asymmetry (C), and/or selection bias from test-set conditioning (E). The methodology as designed cannot support its stated objective.

The critic correctly identifies that the root cause of A and B is shared (the random split prevents enforcing an information boundary), but they are also independently correctible problems: even with a temporal split, graph features would need point-in-time construction. And Critiques C and E are independent of A/B entirely.

This is not a case where empirical testing would resolve the disagreement — the design errors are structural and would need to be fixed before any empirical result could be trusted. The verdict is therefore **critique_wins**.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random splitting allows campaign emails to appear in both training and test sets, causing graph features computed on the full corpus to leak future information into test-set scoring.

Graph features (especially sender reputation and first-time-sender flag) are computed on the full 18-month corpus rather than on only data available before each email's timestamp, creating lookahead contamination.
The joint model receives a full hyperparameter search while the text-only baseline is fixed to a single configuration, conflating feature value with optimization advantage.

Analyst confirmation lag means recent emails are systematically less likely to be labeled positive regardless of true maliciousness, and this temporal bias interacts with the random split in a way that is masked rather than controlled.
The held-out test partition is used to break checkpoint ties during model selection, violating holdout integrity and biasing the reported AUC.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random splitting allows campaign emails to appear in both training and test sets, causing graph features computed on the full corpus to leak future information into test-set scoring — conceded substantially by the defender.

Graph features (especially sender reputation and first-time-sender flag) are computed on the full 18-month corpus rather than on only data available before each email's timestamp, creating lookahead contamination — conceded fully by the defender.

The joint model receives a full hyperparameter search while the text-only baseline is fixed to a single configuration, conflating feature value with optimization advantage — conceded fully by the defender.

Analyst confirmation lag means recent emails are systematically less likely to be labeled positive regardless of true maliciousness, and this temporal bias interacts with the random split in a way that is masked rather than controlled — partially conceded; the comparative AUC may survive if bias affects both models equally, but the diagnostic is absent and the bias is real.
The held-out test partition is used to break checkpoint ties during model selection, violating holdout integrity and biasing the reported AUC — conceded fully and immediately by the defender.
